title: Lists and quotes
assignment: Review the concepts from this section. If something is unclear, go back to the lesson.
groups.0.title: The real list
groups.0.items.0.def:
Dashes and
look like a list. They are not. Try reordering a row or removing one item — every change is manual. A list CLAIMS: these things are of one kind.
groups.0.items.1.def: <ul> wraps the list, <li> is each thing. The browser draws the bullets — you type none. Leave dashes inside and they sit NEXT TO its bullets.
groups.0.items.2.def: <ul> — order does not matter. <ol> — it does: steps, a ranking. The browser numbers <ol> itself, and reorder two items and the numbers rearrange on their own.
groups.1.title: Nested and pairs
groups.1.items.0.def:
A new
    INSIDE an
  • , before you close it — not next to it. That way the sub-list is the thing's content, not its neighbour. The closing
    • goes AFTER the inner
.
groups.1.items.1.def: A list of name-value pairs. <dt> is the name, <dd> is the value. For specs, a glossary, metadata. The colon disappears — the tags separate instead of it.
groups.2.title: Someone else's words
groups.2.items.0.def: Quotation marks are just characters — the computer does not know the words are not yours. <blockquote> is a block quote, <q> is inline. The tag claims it; the marks become redundant. Both take a cite with the source's address.
